text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

Rebecca McGowan will be competing alongside her boyfriend and training with Jade Jones in the biggest summer of her career so far

By Paul Eddison| Rebecca McGowan has always dreamed of competing at the Olympics but to book her place atParis 2024, she had to take down an icon of British Taekwondo.

The 24-year-old from Dumbarton clinched a quota spot forTeam GBlast year, but then had to wait nervously to find out whether she had done enough to take that spot, or whether three-time world champion and double Olympic bronze medallist Bianca Cook (nee Walkden) would be selected in her place.

There was no question that McGowan deserved, ranking higher in the 67+kg category, having won a World Championship silver medal and a pair of European bronzes during this Olympic cycle.

The most recent of those European podiums came in Belgrade in May, with Cook representing the Isle of Man as she did everything she could to stake a claim for the sole Team GB spot.

In Belgrade, however, it was McGowan who finished among the medals, as Cook went out at the quarter-final stage.

Whether that tipped the balance in the final decision from GB Taekwondo remains a mystery, but having finally had her place in Paris confirmed, McGowan has now been able to turn her focus to her Olympic ambitions.

She said: “It’s really exciting. It’s nice to be here amongst it all now. It’s nice to have the final say, you are going, get ready. We’ve all been getting ready all year, but now it’s fully tunnel vision on that, I’m not thinking about anything else.

“I don’t do that (think about what the other person is doing). I imagine you would end up going crazy if you do that. So all I did was focus on me. I focused on what I did in my events and as long as I was putting out the best results I could, that is all I could do. Thankfully, it’s good enough.

“I’ve just been making sure I’m in top shape for Paris. I’ve been focusing on myself, focusing on being the best me that I can be. I’m trying to become a bit of a beast.”

Cook and McGowan know each other well, with the Scot having travelled toTokyothree years ago as a reserve.

In Japan, Cook claimed a second successive bronze medal on the Olympic stage, and admitted that missing out on a final shot at bronze was a devastating blow, having called for a fight-off with McGowan to decide the place.

She told theBBC: “It feels like a massive loss, like grieving, because I’m just absolutely devastated.

“I didn’t expect to just be selected, I wanted to earn that place and I thought that after dominating for so many years and the history that I’ve achieved for the sport, I deserved a fight-off.

“Paris was always going to be my last Olympics and I know in my heart I was capable of turning those shiny [bronze] medals into a nice gold one, so I’m devastated because it feels like my journey has been cut short.”

In a statement, GB Taekwondo explained the reasoning behind the lack of a fight-off.

“The Olympic Selection Panel felt confident that Rebecca McGowan was in a better position than Bianca Cook to contribute to winning the best set of medals for Team GB.”

For McGowan, that Tokyo experience has been one of her main drivers on this Olympic cycle. Even with such fierce competition for the British spot, she was determined not to relive the same scenario.

She said: “Being able to get that experience (in Tokyo) gave me a fire in my belly that I’ve never had before. I’d done almost the entire prep and not being able to do that final part, I wasn’t going to do it again for this one, I was making sure I was going to be the one that was fighting there.”

McGowan is one of over 1,000 elite athletes on UK Sport’s National Lottery-funded World Class Programme, allowing her to train full-time, have access to the world’s best coaches and benefit from pioneering medical support.

In Paris, McGowan will compete alongside her boyfriend and two-time world champion Bradly Sinden, who won silver in Tokyo, as well as fellow Olympic newcomer and housemate Caden Cunningham.

There is inevitably a lot of excitement with the trio making up three-quarters of the taekwondo contingent heading over to France, with double Olympic championJade Jonescompleting the quartet.

McGowan said: “It’s a nice house, we’re all there prepping together, training together. It’s a good house to be in. We’re all in the excited phase, all ready to be there and embrace it all. It’s quite a high-energy house.”

As with many taekwondo athletes, McGowan has suffered her fair share of injuries, including a pair of broken ribs just a fortnight before she claimed her World Championship silver medal.

But with the Olympics now just around the corner, McGowan is doing everything to ensure she arrives in France in the best possible shape.

She said: “I’m feeling good, I’m feeling the best I’ve felt for a long time.

“We’ve already started our prep camp, we’ve got training partners over. We’re in the deep of it right now.

“We’re just continuing on with that, ironing everything out, making sure that everything is as good as it can be. We want to make sure we’re even more beasts for Paris.

“We’re training a lot. For me it has to be smart training. With the injuries I’ve carried, I train a bit smarter, if I’ve only got so many sessions, I’ve got to make the most of everyone. I can’t let any time slide. I’ve got to make the most of every session.”

McGowan will compete on the final Saturday of the Games, along with Cunningham, knowing that her entire Olympic dream rests entirely on a single day.

But rather than worry about what might happen if she is not at her best on the day, McGowan is going to seize an opportunity.

She concluded: “It’s something that is inevitable, you will think about it. It’s four years’ worth of work for one day. But I’ve tried to switch my thought process around it. Hopefully, it could be done in six minutes, so give everything you can in those six minutes. And then you could walk away Olympic champion.”

National Lottery players raise more than £30million a week for good causes including vital funding into sport – from grassroots to elite. To find out more visit:www.lotterygoodcauses.org.uk#TNLAthletes

title: The Team GB Taekwondo star 'trying to become a beast' at Paris 2024
author: Sportsbeat
description: Rebecca McGowan will be competing alongside her boyfriend and training with Jade Jones in the biggest summer of her career so far
